text: The left lower quadrant of the abdomen showing adhesions due to diverticulitis.
The small bowel is adherent to the sigmoid as well as to the anti-abdominal wall.
Adhesions are carefully separated, maintaining the hemostasis.
The small bowel is separated from the anti-abdominal wall and is detached from the inflamed sigmoid colon.
The sigmoid colon was then separated from the anti-abdominal wall
There were thick fibrous adhesions between the sigmoid and the anti-abdominal wall and it was carefully separated, avoiding any opening in the sigmoid colon to prevent fecal contamination.
One can see the inflamed diverticula in the sigmoid colon being separated out.
The tinea coli of the sigmoid colon is visible.
Once the sigmoid is free from the anti-abdominal wall, dissection of the right leaf of the mesosigmoid is begun.
Sacral promontory is identified and above the sacral promontory, the leaf of the mesocolon is divided.
The division continues downwards.
The aim is to identify the sigmoid branches of the inferior mesenteric artery.
careful slow patient dissection will show the sigmoid branches.
Generally, there are three sigmoid branches arising out of the inferior mesenteric artery and each one needs to be clipped and divided close to the colon.
The first sigmoid branch from the infiemicentric artery is seen and the infiemicentric artery is pushed downwards.
The branch is further cleared all around and clipped.
The branch is divided using harmonic shears.
In the same way, the remaining two sigmoid branches are also isolated, identified and divided.
the dissection continues downwards up to the rectosigmoid junction to the proposed site of division.
The dissection continues downwards posteriorly and the mesosigmoid is taken up to the rectosigmoid junction or the third piece of the sacrum.
Preserving the inferior mesenteric artery maintains the blood supply to the upper rectum and decreases the chances of the anastomotic leak.
The posterior wall of the recto sigmoid is seen.
Once it is ensured that the caudal end of the dissection includes all the diverticuli, the peritoneal incision is turned anteriorly and to the left to divide the mesocolon anteriorly.
In sigmoidectomy for benign disease, one does not have to dissect very deep posteriorly.
The muscular fibers of the anterior surface of the rectosigmoid are seen.
The pulsations of the infirmary centric artery which has been carefully preserved can be noted.
Once this part of the dissection is over, attention is turned to the peritoneum on the left lateral pelvic wall.
The peritoneum at the rectosigmoid junction is incised and is carried upwards in the left paracolic gutter, mobilizing the lower portion of the left colon.
The dissection is then carried downwards.
The gonadal vessels are identified and it is very crucial to identify the left ureter and safeguard it.
The uretic peristalsis can be noted.
The incision is continued on the left of the rectosigmoid towards the pelvis.
The posterior dissection of this area has already been done from the right side.
The incision is carried downwards to join the incision which has been made from the right side.
The posterior surface of the rectosigmoid now stands completely mobilized and free.

once the recto sigmoid is dissected circumferentially and all the fat is removed an echelon 60 stapler with a green load is used to divide the recto sigmoid the colon is then exteriorized through an incision in the left lact fossa the specimen is excised the anvil of the stapler is placed a purse string applied and then the colon is pushed in
A 33 French SDH stapler is brought trans-anally.
The spike is advanced to come inside through the staple line.
The rectal wall is supported when the spike is coming in.
The anvil of the stapler at the sigmoid colon is locked into place.
Both the ends of the sigmoid and the rectum are approximated by closing the stapler, making sure that no fat or intestine is lodged between the two ends.
Once the approximation is ensured,
And the stapler line is in the green range.
The stapler is activated to complete the anastomosis.
The stapler is gradually removed transiently.
The anastomosis is checked and tested by injecting saline from below.
